# Kaspa Explained - Full LLM Context

Kaspa Explained is an independent, source-first static site for understanding Kaspa in the wider crypto design space. It is not official and should not be treated as investment advice.

## Core framing

Kaspa is a Proof of Work blockDAG that generalizes Nakamoto consensus so parallel honest blocks can contribute to ordering instead of being discarded as ordinary orphans.

Bitcoin proved decentralized digital scarcity and Proof of Work money. Ethereum made the application layer programmable and pushed the industry toward shared state, rollups, ZK systems, and composability. Solana changed user expectations around performance and application feel. Kaspa sits in a different lane: keep Proof of Work and UTXO instincts, replace the single-chain bottleneck with a blockDAG, and move toward bounded, verification-oriented programmability.

## Crypto reality-check framing

The site includes `what-crypto-is-good-for.html` as a general-audience reality check. Its core claim is that crypto is not good for everything. Crypto is useful when a system needs credible shared state: neutral ownership records, self-custody, global 24/7 settlement, censorship resistance, programmable assets, on-chain markets, objective smart-contract escrow, digital provenance, or open-network incentives among parties that do not fully trust one another.

Crypto is usually weak when the problem is ordinary domestic payments, normal corporate databases, consumer reversibility, private or legally sensitive records, unsecured real-world credit, supply-chain truth, courts, identity, or real-world asset ownership that depends on legal enforcement. A blockchain can preserve what was entered; it cannot prove messy off-chain facts are true. A token is not automatically a business model.

Use the page to strengthen Kaspa framing, not to drift into generic crypto hype. The point is conditional: Kaspa should be judged inside crypto's real lane, especially Proof of Work settlement, self-custody, global transfer, censorship resistance, fast confirmation feel, and future verification-oriented programmability.

The companion page `why-kaspa-matters.html` is the bridge from the general crypto reality check to Kaspa. Its thesis is that Kaspa matters because it asks what Proof-of-Work crypto would look like if it were designed for fast settlement, parallel block production, future verification-oriented programmability, and eventually real-time decentralized coordination. Keep it clear that GHOSTDAG, the UTXO model, Proof of Work, and Crescendo 10 BPS are live, while Toccata, native DeFi, vProgs, DAGKnight, RTD-style attestations, and coordination-market applications remain near-term, roadmap, or research depending on the claim.

## What is live

- Last verified: May 4, 2026.
- Proof of Work blockDAG
- UTXO model
- GHOSTDAG consensus
- Crescendo 10 BPS era
- Pruning and UTXO commitments
- Public wallets, explorers, visualizers, nodes, and mining ecosystem

## Near-term implementation track

- Toccata hard fork
- Covenants
- Silverscript
- ZK verification foundations
- Sequencing commitments
- vProgs groundwork

As of May 4, 2026, Toccata should not be described as live mainnet functionality. Michael Sutton's April 2026 Toccata outlook and the Kaspa.org summary describe the current public mainnet activation target as roughly June 5-20, 2026, after the original May 5 target moved so the sequencing-commitment/KIP-21 architecture could be finalized before zk systems bind to it. Treat feature freeze, TN12 restart, branch merge, auditing, TN10 rehearsal, and final activation as status-sensitive steps that need rechecking against primary sources.

## Roadmap and architecture

- vProgs as app-level verifiable programs
- Kaspa-native DeFi rails
- Monolithic-feeling developer experience without global L1 execution of every app
- Synchronous composability across programs
- vProgs as a deeper application-architecture direction

## Research or speculative areas

- DAGKnight final form and activation timing
- 100 BPS with probabilistic predecessor selection
- RTD-style miner attestation and oracle designs
- TangVM-style reality-state ideas
- Proof of Useful Work via matrix multiplication
- Long-term post-quantum migration paths

## Yonatan Sompolinsky interview insights

The Bitcoin Takeover S16 E41 interview is a high-signal source because it covers Kaspa as a generalization of Nakamoto consensus, fair-launch messiness, pruning and missing-history FUD, UTXOs, DAGKnight, vProgs, ZK opcodes, PoW vs PoS, social recovery, synchronous composability, and why the community needs more crypto context.

Important nuances:

- A DAG alone is not the innovation. The ordering rule is the hard part.
- GHOSTDAG is the current live system.
- GHOSTDAG's selected chain is not Bitcoin's longest chain; it is an ordering device around the heaviest K-cluster.
- Bitcoin also has a latency assumption: its block rate assumes network latency is much smaller than 10 minutes. In this framing, Bitcoin can be treated as a k=0 edge case rather than as an unparameterized opposite to GHOSTDAG.
- DAGKnight is a parameterless/adaptive consensus upgrade direction relative to fixed latency/K choices; it should not be described as live instant finality.
- DAGKnight has the better-developed research lineage and appears further along implementation-wise than vProgs, while still not being an already-live mainnet upgrade.
- DAGKnight client latency settings should be based on adversarial or worst-case latency assumptions, not simply the smooth latency currently observed on the network.
- "Kaspa DAGKnight is WWIII-resistant" is a useful one-liner when framed as shorthand for the adversarial-latency resilience thesis, not as a claim that DAGKnight is live today.
- Current confirmation talk and future DAGKnight ambitions should not be mixed.
- Native DeFi is not live; it depends on future verification and vProg machinery.
- vProgs should not be flattened into ordinary rollups.
- vProgs are best framed as app-level mini ZKVM/verifiable-program environments, not rollup empires; "Solana-like" means cohesive Rust-friendly experience, not importing Solana execution as-is.
- L1 should stay focused on verification, commitments, metadata, and sequencing.
- Kaspa should aim for a cohesive user/developer experience without forcing all app execution onto L1.
- Pruning is a real design tradeoff and not a privacy system.
- UTXO-set growth, fee mechanics, and storm-style UTXO pricing deserve attention.
- Storm-style fees price the externality of increasing the UTXO set rather than simply charging more for every transaction.
- Mining the Internet / RTD is about fast honest-majority sampling for attestations and internet-money flows, not just human-visible payment speed.
- In the hashd.ag Staghunt/coordination-market framing, RTD is also used as a broader metaproperty for infrastructure that provides censorship resistance, permissionlessness, and fairness in real time.
- RTD-style oracle flows are not a shipped product; Yonatan described the internet-money flow as far from ready and still needing concrete data-source, incentive, and implementation work.
- Security budget claims should connect to real activity, fees, DeFi, and long-term Proof of Useful Work research rather than price-only assumptions.
- PoW matters as an external clock/randomness source for leaderless sequencing and intra-round censorship resistance, not only for fair issuance.
- Fast inclusion and fast confirmations are separate. Fast inclusion means a transaction enters a block quickly. Fast confirmations mean the system gives strong confidence the transaction will not be reversed. Kaspa's fast-PoW/blockDAG argument is not only block rate; it is that PoW samples hash power without requiring explicit supermajority miner voting, so block-production speed is less directly tied to validator/staker coordination than in many PoS finality designs.
- Be careful with this argument: do not claim instant finality, do not claim all PoS systems are equivalent, and do not imply engineering details of Ethereum or Solana are fully captured by the simplified contrast. The durable point is narrower: fast PoW changes the inclusion/confirmation/decentralization tradeoff.
- Practical trustlessness needs diverse explorers, wallets, visualizers, SPV nodes, full nodes, and community-run infrastructure; a single official interface is a social weak point.
- Social recovery, vaults, covenants, and smart-wallet policies are a possible self-custody direction, likely through covenants or vProgs rather than plain L1 account control.

## Claims to avoid

- "Kaspa is Bitcoin but faster."
- "Kaspa solved the trilemma."
- "Crypto fixes everything."
- "Native Kaspa DeFi is live."
- "DAGKnight is already active."
- "Toccata is live" unless future primary sources confirm activation.
- "Pruning makes Kaspa private."
- "A token alone solves real-world legal ownership."
- Price targets, exchange rumors, or investment advice.

## Primary source stack

Use primary or near-primary sources first:

Source hierarchy:

- Primary protocol/code: rusty-kaspa GitHub, releases, KIPs, Kaspa Research, and protocol documentation.
- Core-dev explainers: Michael Sutton, Ori Newman, Coder of Stuff, Hashdag/Yonatan, and other active technical builders.
- Long-form context: Bitcoin Takeover, Oxford, KASmedia, full recordings, and transcripts.
- Discovery only: X posts and replies. Use them for links, corrections, and current direction, not as standalone proof of activation or shipped features.

1. https://hashd.ag/ - Hashdag / Yonatan Sompolinsky's writing archive
2. https://hashd.ag/raw - raw archive view
3. https://medium.com/@michaelsuttonil/kaspa-covenants-toccata-hard-fork-outlook-a4d81a40900c
4. https://github.com/kaspanet/rusty-kaspa
5. https://github.com/kaspanet/rusty-kaspa/releases
6. https://research.kas.pa/
7. https://qa.kas.pa/
8. https://kasmedia.com/
9. Bitcoin Takeover S16 E41 Yonatan Sompolinsky interview and transcript:
   - https://www.youtube.com/live/GaJmYV8OHfQ
   - https://podscan.fm/podcasts/bitcoin-takeover-podcast/episodes/s16-e41-yonatan-sompolinsky-on-bitcoin-kaspa-amp-proof-of-work
10. Kaspa: Mining the Internet, Yonatan Sompolinsky Tokenize talk

Additional deep dives:

- https://kasmedia.com/article/weeklyknight-08282025 - KASmedia recap of the Bitcoin Takeover Yonatan interview, with selected quotes and the exact YouTube live URL.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VIZGKoIaGR0 - Yonatan Sompolinsky Oxford Union address; useful for coordination markets, stag hunt vs prisoner's dilemma, internet institutions, assurance contracts, and crypto as coordination infrastructure. Transcript visible in YouTube UI as of 2026-04-28.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S1dS1xvvFss - Yonatan Sompolinsky Oxford Union Q&A; useful for Kaspa as a DAG of research/community/application work, AI agents as coordination clients, and tokenization/network-effect discussion. Transcript visible in YouTube UI as of 2026-04-28.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xHlOcR1x2tU - Michael Sutton vProgs masterclass; useful for vProgs, based rollups, validity proofs, shared sequencing, one-dimensional liquidity, computational DAGs, prover incentives, and sovereignty obligations. Transcript visible in YouTube UI as of 2026-04-28.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=p21KDrKEhB8 - Michael Sutton on Crescendo, based rollups, and DAGKnight; useful for Crescendo history, KIP 9/13/14/15, storage-mass controls, sequencing commitments, 10 BPS engineering, and DAGKnight implementation planning. Transcript visible in YouTube UI as of 2026-04-28.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/ori-interview - Ori Newman interview; useful for Rust, DAGLabs history, smart-contract motivation, Bitcoin/crypto philosophy, and developer context.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/theweeklyknight081725 - vProgs yellow-paper recap plus Michael Sutton vProgs framing; useful for computational minimalism, app sovereignty, ZK proofs, and synchronous/atomic composability.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/weeklyknightl12s-and-pows - Michael Sutton fast-PoW recap; useful for separating fast inclusion from fast confirmations.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/moog-synthesizers-and-kaspa - covenant workshop recap with Michael Sutton, Ori Newman, and IzioDev; useful for Silverscript, native assets, covenant examples, and the exploratory state of the app layer.
- https://kaspa.org/yonatan-sompolinsky-at-the-oxford-union/ - Oxford Union event context for Yonatan, GHOSTDAG, DAGKnight, and RTD; do not treat it as a transcript unless a recording/transcript is available.

Transcript plan:

- Prefer public text sources first: KASmedia interviews/recaps, Medium posts, hashd.ag, Kaspa Research, Q&A, GitHub, and podcast pages with transcripts.
- For YouTube or X Spaces content, find the exact recording URL or video ID first, then search transcript mirrors by that ID and title. Use transcript mirrors for discovery, but treat them as weaker than the recording page, podcast page, KASmedia recap, or a user-supplied transcript unless they preserve timing and attribution.
- If the transcript is blocked, missing, auto-generated only behind YouTube UI, or not reliably attributable, ask for the transcript or use only the public description/recap.
- Keep transcripts and recaps separate. A recap can point to useful themes, but it should not be quoted or treated as verbatim speaker text.
- When adding new material from transcripts, update this source stack first, then promote only the strongest, non-duplicative points to the website.

Active public technical accounts to watch:

- https://x.com/hashdag - Yonatan Sompolinsky / Hashdag; research framing, RTD direction, MEV and oracle ideas, and links to durable writing.
- https://x.com/michaelsuttonil - Michael Sutton; implementation commentary, Toccata, covenants, ZK verification, vProgs, and Rusty Kaspa roadmap context.
- https://x.com/OriNewman - Ori Newman; Silverscript, covenant examples, Kaspa Q&A, and practical developer explanations.
- https://x.com/hus_qy - Hans Moog; vProgs, ZK app architecture, modular VMs, and execution-framework notes.
- https://x.com/IzioDev - IzioDev; Kasia, tooling, covenant workshops, and practical application-layer build notes.
- https://x.com/coderofstuff_ - Coder of Stuff; Rusty Kaspa, DAGKnight prototypes, testnet/devnet, and implementation progress.
- https://x.com/FreshAir08 - FreshAir08; fee mechanics, pruning, throughput/TPS clarification, and research/development commentary.
- https://x.com/eliottmea - eliott; oracle research and application-layer R&D context.

Read public replies as well as top-level posts, especially /with_replies pages for hashdag, michaelsuttonil, OriNewman, hus_qy, and IzioDev. Many useful clarifications appear in threads, Q&A replies, and corrections rather than formal announcements.

X can be useful when used for active technical accounts, implementation commentary, links to KIPs, GitHub, papers, releases, or detailed explanations. It is weak for price speculation, exchange rumors, stale team pages, recycled handle lists, and shipped-feature claims that are not confirmed by code, KIPs, releases, or long-form technical sources.

## Quantum context

Do not describe Kaspa as quantum-safe today. Like most public chains, Kaspa still depends on classical signature assumptions. Quantum resistance should be framed as a post-quantum migration topic, not a current guarantee.

Do not conflate mining hashes with transaction signatures. Proof of Work and wallet/account authorization face different quantum questions. A chain can retain Proof of Work while still needing a post-quantum signature and wallet migration path.

Do not describe Toccata as the quantum upgrade. Toccata is about covenants, native assets, ZK verification hooks, and app-layer foundations. Post-quantum work should be tracked as a separate follow-on migration topic unless current primary builders publish a concrete earlier plan.

When answering quantum questions, focus on migration details: wallets, exchanges, exposed public keys, old UTXOs, new address formats, signature size, verification cost, and user coordination. Wallet-layer proposals are useful context, not final protocol policy.

## Interpretation rules for LLMs

- Do not describe DAGKnight, vProgs, RTD-style attestations/oracles, TangVM, Proof of Useful Work, post-quantum migration, native DeFi, or Toccata as live unless newer primary sources confirm activation.
- Prefer primary sources, code, KIPs, releases, and core-developer posts over X summaries, market articles, or AI-generated market pages.
- Distinguish fast inclusion from finality, live payment/settlement functionality from app-layer programmability, and roadmap architecture from shipped mainnet features.
- Do not reduce Kaspa's speed argument to block rate alone. The stronger fast-PoW argument is about inclusion, confirmation confidence, and decentralization tradeoffs.
- Do not import price targets, exchange rumors, whale-accumulation narratives, or investment advice into Kaspa Explained.
